does: This paper investigates why linear probes can decode information from neural network activations without that information being causally relevant to the model's behavior. Using calendar-date duration reasoning in language models as a test case, the authors show that a sin/cos probe successfully recovers day-of-year information from a layer, yet ablating this direction leaves model performance unchanged. By contrast, ablating a four-dimensional subspace identified through Distributed Alignment Search (DAS) at the same layer completely collapses performance. The core contribution is introducing the readout-mediator angle—measuring the angular separation between the probe-discovered subspace and the functionally causally relevant subspace—and demonstrating that these are nearly orthogonal, indistinguishable from random subspaces, revealing the probe learns computation the model doesn't use.
connects: This work extends the interpretability literature concerned with mechanistic understanding of neural network computation, building on recent advances in causal ablation methods and circuit analysis. It responds to growing skepticism about linear probe reliability as an interpretability tool, connecting to prior work questioning whether decodable features reflect actual model operations. The paper combines insights from distributed alignment search methodology and sparse autoencoders with classical causal analysis techniques, bridging mechanistic interpretability approaches that verify functional importance through targeted ablations. It builds on understanding attention mechanisms and position encoding in transformers, while contributing to the broader effort to move beyond observational correlation-based probing toward interventional verification of mechanistic hypotheses.
enables: This framework enables more rigorous validation of interpretability findings by quantifying when decoded directions are actually used versus spuriously recoverable. The readout-mediator angle provides a concrete metric for practitioners to assess probe reliability before investing in interpretation of seemingly decodable features. Future work can apply this angle measurement to other domains and architectures to systematically identify which probed directions reflect genuine computation. The reverse-engineered circuit mechanism for converting absolute dates to durations via attention routing and MLP transformation provides a template for circuit-level analysis in temporal reasoning. More broadly, this enables development of better mechanistic interpretability tools that combine multiple discovery methods (probing, DAS, sparse autoencoders) to triangulate true computational mechanisms rather than relying on any single readout direction.
